message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

Resume final debugging of JJK pace ₢BYAAA (heat ₣BY, rbk-08-credential-repairs,
  silks "lifecycle-split-establish-rekey"). Open an officium, then `jjx_orient`
  firemark BY and read the paddock + docket before acting.

  STATUS: the pace is CODE-COMPLETE and committed. Only live validation/debugging
  remains. Nothing to design — the next step is to verify a fix landed live, then
  propose wrap (never auto-wrap; the operator decides).

  WHAT THE PACE DID (H1 + H2 in the keyfile credential tier):
  - H1 (binding leak): divest now revokes a role's own IAM bindings BEFORE deleting
    the SA, so no `deleted:...?uid=` tombstones accrue. New lean revoke layer in
    rbgi_IAM.sh (rbgi_revoke_project_member/_repo_member/_sa_member + shared
    rbgi_jq_remove_member_from_role_capture); wired into rbgg_divest_director/
    _retriever before zrbgg_divest_role.
  - H2 (re-key churns the SA): zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key is now
    idempotent (preflight 200 → keep SA+uid+bindings, rotate key only; 404 →
    create) with an automated key blunt-kill (delete all USER_MANAGED keys, mint
    one). Rust rbtdrk_role_invest_impl had its pre-divest removed (invest is
    idempotent now).
  - The revoke functions are REGULAR fatal functions (buc_die on failure) — NOT
    best-effort; subshells are disallowed for that. Divest-revoke is fatal by design.

  COMMITS THIS HEAT (newest last):
  - 9f0beef9  bash+rust H1/H2
  - 6901d2f8  specs aligned (RBSRK/RBSDK idempotent, RBSRD/RBSDD revoke-then-delete,
              RBSCIG revoke contract, RBSCIP race relocation)
  - 00dea588  added a divest→reinvest "recycle" to the SHARED canonical-invest case
              list (rbtdrk_canonical.rs), so skirmish/dogfight/blockade now exercise
              the divest path + the IAM delete→recreate eventual-consistency edge
              every run
  - 1a66443e  THE FIX TO VERIFY: restored Class-C (403 caller-recently-empowered)
              propagation tolerance to the resource-scope revokes
  (Earlier in the heat, a first attempt was backed out at f94f61e25 and reslated.)

  IMMEDIATE NEXT STEP — verify commit 1a66443e live:
  Run `tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh canonical-invest` (background; ~minutes, hits live
  GCP IAM). It runs governor_mantle → director_divest → retriever_divest →
  retriever_invest → director_invest against the STANDING depot. On the PREVIOUS
  run it FAILED at director_divest: the just-re-mantled governor's roles/owner had
  not propagated to the AR resource-scope IAM cache → HTTP 403 on the repo
  getIamPolicy. The lean revoke had dropped ALL propagation tolerance; the fix adds
  a Class-C-only (403) GET-retry to rbgi_revoke_repo_member and
  rbgi_revoke_sa_member (project revoke + both setIamPolicy loops stay lean — same
  roles/owner gates read and write; member-visibility classes A/B can't fire on a
  revoke). If it fails again, read the captured stderr under the trace dir
  (.../rbtd/rbtdrk_director_divest/divest-director-stdout.txt) — the tabtarget self-
  logs there and to ../logs-buk/.

  NOTE: director-canest-dir is currently in a PARTIAL state from the failed divest
  (project + Mason-SA bindings revoked, repo binding intact, SA not deleted). The
  canonical-invest rerun's divest cleans it up (re-revokes the intact repo binding,
  no-ops the already-gone ones, deletes, reinvests). Don't separately repair it.

  AFTER canonical-invest is GREEN — the no-tombstone assertion (H1's actual proof):
  verify with gcloud, independent of the kit. Mint a payor OAuth token and read the
    PROJECT=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
    client_id/token_uri from ../station-files/secrets/client_secrets/client_secret_*.json (.installed.*)
    RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET + RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN from ../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env
    POST grant_type=refresh_token to the token_uri → access_token
    CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN=<token> gcloud ... --project=$PROJECT
  Confirm: both canonical SAs (director-canest-dir, retriever-canest-ret) present,
  exactly ONE USER_MANAGED key each (`gcloud iam service-accounts keys list
  --managed-by=user`), and grep the project getIamPolicy for any
  `deleted:...?uid=` ghost naming canest-dir/canest-ret — there should be no NEW
  one from the divest (revoke removed the live member before delete). gcloud is at
  /usr/bin/gcloud; the kit itself self-credentials via the governor RBRA JWT, so
  gcloud is only for independent verification.

  ENVIRONMENT CONSTRAINTS:
  - DO NOT run anything that creates/destroys a GCP depot project. So gauntlet and
    the pristine-lifecycle fixture are OFF LIMITS (they levy/unmake a throwaway
    project and require a marshal-zero baseline this repo isn't in).
  - The standing canonical depot is cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001. Suites that exercise
    this code via the standing depot: canonical-invest (targeted), skirmish, dogfight
    (canonical-invest + a ~330s Cloud Build), blockade. dogfight already passed once
    pre-fix on the invest side; the divest path is the new coverage.
  - Run fixtures sequentially, never parallel. Never pipe tabtargets; read
    ../logs-buk/last.txt after. Run tabtargets in the background for long live runs.

  COLLABORATION DISCIPLINE (the operator holds to these — honor them):
  - Before writing ANY bash, read BCG (Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md)
    IN FULL. Before ANY Rust, read RCG (Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md)
    IN FULL. Earlier in this heat the agent skimmed and was corrected hard — don't.
  - Lean BUT load-bearing: drop machinery that can't fire, keep what provably can.
    The Class-C bug came from over-leaning (dropping a tolerance that DOES fire).
  - Comments describe code permanently, never narrate the change (no "used to…",
    no pace/coronet refs).
  - Sincerity over smoothness; surface real findings; ask before reversing a
    jointly-made decision or doing anything depot-mutating.

  DONE CRITERIA STILL OPEN: live service-tier validation (the canonical-invest
  rerun + the no-tombstone gcloud check). Everything else (shellcheck 186 clean,
  theurge build + 137 unit tests) is green. Once the live checks pass, report and
  ask the operator whether to wrap ₢BYAAA.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Surgical, security-sensitive credential refactor — NOT mechanical. Live GCP IAM;
  verification is expensive and intermittent. Survey before adding, cite BCG before
  writing bash, work one small increment at a time with operator review.
  
  **Read first:** BCG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) — especially the
  load-bearing-complexity example that forbids a generic IAM helper. Grounding:
  `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`. No IAM
  member-removal exists in the kit today; the grant pattern (GET -> modify ->
  setIamPolicy) lives in rbgi/rbga/rbgg — survey it before adding revoke.
  
  Close two holes. **H1:** divest never revokes IAM bindings -> `deleted:` ghost
  tombstones accrue unbounded. **H2:** re-credentialing deletes+recreates the whole
  SA when it should rotate the key only.
  
  **Surgical, in place. Do NOT split functions or rewrite the invest verbs**
  (`rbgg_invest_director` / `rbgg_invest_retriever`) — their grant sequences stay
  byte-for-byte and re-run idempotently on re-invest.
  
  ### Bash — rbgg_Governor.sh
  - CHANGE `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key` (keep the name) to idempotent:
    SA-exists preflight skips create instead of `buc_die`; guard create+poll on the
    absent branch; any cross-branch local declared at function scope with explicit init.
  - ADD automated key blunt-kill: replace the manual-console-abort on existing
    USER_MANAGED keys with delete-all-then-mint-one (new key-delete infix; reuse rbgg's
    existing jq-name-extraction idiom).
  - CHANGE `rbgg_divest_director` / `rbgg_divest_retriever` to revoke their own bindings
    before `zrbgg_divest_role` deletes the SA (director: project + Mason-SA + repo;
    retriever: project).
  
  ### Bash — rbgi_IAM.sh
  - ADD per-scope member-revoke (project, SA, repo): three self-contained functions
    mirroring `rbgi_add_*_iam_role` one-for-one, NOT a generic helper (BCG load-bearing).
    Share only a jq member-removal helper paralleling `rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture`.
  
  ### Rust — rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs
  - DELETE the pre-divest in `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (the divest-then-invest dance was
    H2's test-side scar); drop the now-unused divest_colophon param and fix callers.
  
  ### Specs — Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/*.adoc
  - RBSRK / RBSDK (invest): fail-loud-on-existing -> idempotent establish + key rotation.
  - RBSRD / RBSDD (divest): SA-delete-only -> teardown (revoke, then delete).
  - RBSCIG: add the parallel revoke contract.
  - RBSCIP: create->grant race relocates to first-establish; delete->recreate race eliminated.
  
  ### Settle at mount (cinch with operator)
  - Revoke in divest: best-effort-with-loud-log vs fatal. Lean best-effort, so a flaky
    revoke cannot regress the working divest path.
  
  ### Out of scope (cinched)
  - One-time sweep of existing ghost bindings.
  - New operator verbs / colophons (one idempotent invest; tabtarget surface unchanged).
  - RBRA one-file-per-person (that is pace ₢BYAAB).
  
  ### Done
  - Standing-depot re-invest rotates the key without SA delete/recreate (no tombstones);
    divest revokes before delete; bash BCG- + shellcheck-clean (`tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh`);
    theurge builds + unit tests green; live service-tier validation operator-driven.

Regenerate tabtarget-context doc and Rust colophon consts via theurge build.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
ec558c24b  ₢BYAAC    Document the intermittent dogfight ordain failure observed during verification: rbfd_ordain's build-status poll (zrbfc_wait_build_completion) aborted a healthy in-flight Cloud Build after 3 consecutive curl rc=28 connect-timeouts (~45s station-side network blip), conflating transport-unreachability with build failure. Self-healed on rerun (run 2 same path passed). Recommends splitting ZRBFC_BUILD_POLL_RETRY_TOLERANCE into a small body-error tolerance (keep 3, fail fast) and a larger transport-failure tolerance (~8, ride out blips), since status-poll reachability is not build health. # jjezs_paddock ₣BY

# Paddock: rbk-08-credential-repairs

## Context

(Describe the initiative's background and goals)

## References

(List relevant files, docs, or prior work)


# jjezs_pace ₢BYAAA

## Character
Surgical, security-sensitive credential refactor — NOT mechanical. Live GCP IAM;
verification is expensive and intermittent. Survey before adding, cite BCG before
writing bash, work one small increment at a time with operator review.

**Read first:** BCG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) — especially the
load-bearing-complexity example that forbids a generic IAM helper. Grounding:
`Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`. No IAM
member-removal exists in the kit today; the grant pattern (GET -> modify ->
setIamPolicy) lives in rbgi/rbga/rbgg — survey it before adding revoke.

Close two holes. **H1:** divest never revokes IAM bindings -> `deleted:` ghost
tombstones accrue unbounded. **H2:** re-credentialing deletes+recreates the whole
SA when it should rotate the key only.

**Surgical, in place. Do NOT split functions or rewrite the invest verbs**
(`rbgg_invest_director` / `rbgg_invest_retriever`) — their grant sequences stay
byte-for-byte and re-run idempotently on re-invest.

### Bash — rbgg_Governor.sh
- CHANGE `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key` (keep the name) to idempotent:
  SA-exists preflight skips create instead of `buc_die`; guard create+poll on the
  absent branch; any cross-branch local declared at function scope with explicit init.
- ADD automated key blunt-kill: replace the manual-console-abort on existing
  USER_MANAGED keys with delete-all-then-mint-one (new key-delete infix; reuse rbgg's
  existing jq-name-extraction idiom).
- CHANGE `rbgg_divest_director` / `rbgg_divest_retriever` to revoke their own bindings
  before `zrbgg_divest_role` deletes the SA (director: project + Mason-SA + repo;
  retriever: project).

### Bash — rbgi_IAM.sh
- ADD per-scope member-revoke (project, SA, repo): three self-contained functions
  mirroring `rbgi_add_*_iam_role` one-for-one, NOT a generic helper (BCG load-bearing).
  Share only a jq member-removal helper paralleling `rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture`.

### Rust — rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs
- DELETE the pre-divest in `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (the divest-then-invest dance was
  H2's test-side scar); drop the now-unused divest_colophon param and fix callers.

### Specs — Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/*.adoc
- RBSRK / RBSDK (invest): fail-loud-on-existing -> idempotent establish + key rotation.
- RBSRD / RBSDD (divest): SA-delete-only -> teardown (revoke, then delete).
- RBSCIG: add the parallel revoke contract.
- RBSCIP: create->grant race relocates to first-establish; delete->recreate race eliminated.

### Settle at mount (cinch with operator)
- Revoke in divest: best-effort-with-loud-log vs fatal. Lean best-effort, so a flaky
  revoke cannot regress the working divest path.

### Out of scope (cinched)
- One-time sweep of existing ghost bindings.
- New operator verbs / colophons (one idempotent invest; tabtarget surface unchanged).
- RBRA one-file-per-person (that is pace ₢BYAAB).

### Done
- Standing-depot re-invest rotates the key without SA delete/recreate (no tombstones);
  divest revokes before delete; bash BCG- + shellcheck-clean (`tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh`);
  theurge builds + unit tests green; live service-tier validation operator-driven.

content.text:
Paddock read — it's a thin placeholder; the docket carries the shape. I have the goal: H1 (divest revokes before delete, no tombstones) and H2 (re-key rotates the key, doesn't churn the SA), and I'm here only to land the live proof for the Class-C fix in `1a66443e`.

One thing I'll flag rather than smooth over: the docket's "Settle at mount" line leaned **best-effort** for divest-revoke, but the handoff tells me the implemented-and-committed decision was **fatal by design**. That's a jointly-made reversal that already landed in `9f0beef9`/`1a66443e` — I'm treating it as cinched, not reopening it. Just naming that the docket prose and the shipped code diverge there.

Let me confirm the tree is clean and HEAD is where I expect before running anything live.

content.text:
The divest invocation log is tiny (662 bytes) and the revoke's 403-retry didn't log — so the divest bash itself ran fast and returned cleanly. That raises a real question I want to answer honestly before claiming the fix is *proven*: the ~90s the engine showed for `director_divest` may have been engine-side spacing, not the revoke's retry loop. If the governor's `roles/owner` happened to propagate fast this run, the divest GET would've returned 200 on the first try and the Class-C tolerance never fired — meaning this run shows divest *works*, but doesn't independently prove the restored retry is load-bearing.

content.text:
That's the proof I was looking for — and it's stronger than "just passed." The transcript shows the Class-C tolerance **firing and being load-bearing this run**:

- The **repo-scoped** revoke (`roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin`) hit `getIamPolicy` → **403 at 0s, 3s, 9s, 21s, 41s, 61s** (seven consecutive 403s, "caller-empowerment propagating"), then **200 at 81s** → setIamPolicy 200, revoked. That ~81s *is* the 90s the engine showed. Pre-fix, this died on the first 403.
- The **project-scope** and **Mason-SA-scope** revokes got **200 on the first GET** — no retry needed. That vindicates the "lean but load-bearing" call exactly: the AR repo resource-scope IAM cache lags, the project/SA gates (same `roles/owner` already held) don't, so the tolerance belongs on the resource-scope revokes only.
- Then DELETE 200 → read-gone confirm loop (3×404 over 6s) → "durably gone." H1 end-to-end: live members revoked *first*, then delete → no tombstone.

Gate 1 is fully proven. Now Gate 2 — the independent gcloud no-tombstone check. Let me locate the secret files first.

echo "=== rbro.env ==="; ls -la /home/bhyslop/projects/station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env 2>/dev/null

input.command:
set -euo pipefail
PROJECT=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
CS_JSON=/home/bhyslop/projects/station-files/secrets/client_secrets/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json
token_uri=$(jq -r '.installed.token_uri' "$CS_JSON")
client_id=$(jq -r '.installed.client_id' "$CS_JSON")
# shellcheck disable=SC1091
source /home/bhyslop/projects/station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env
access_token=$(curl -s --request POST "$token_uri" \
  --data-urlencode "client_id=${client_id}" \
  --data-urlencode "client_secret=${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
  --data-urlencode "refresh_token=${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" \
  --data-urlencode "grant_type=refresh_token" | jq -r '.access_token')
export CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN="$access_token"

for sa in director-canest-dir retriever-canest-ret; do
  email="${sa}@${PROJECT}.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
  n=$(/usr/bin/gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --project="$PROJECT" \
        --iam-account="$email" --managed-by=user --format="value(name)" 2>/dev/null | grep -c .)
  echo "(b) ${sa}: USER_MANAGED key count = ${n}"
done
echo
echo "=== (c) project IAM policy: any deleted:...?uid= ghost naming canest-dir/canest-ret ? ==="
ghosts=$(/usr/bin/gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$PROJECT" --format=json 2>/dev/null \
  | jq -r '.bindings[].members[]' | grep -E '^deleted:' | grep -E 'canest-dir|canest-ret' || true)
if [ -z "$ghosts" ]; then
  echo "NONE — no deleted: tombstone names the canonical director/retriever SAs"
else
  echo "FOUND tombstone(s):"; printf '%s\n' "$ghosts"
fi
echo
echo "=== (c-context) ALL deleted: members in project policy (for completeness) ==="
/usr/bin/gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$PROJECT" --format=json 2>/dev/null \
  | jq -r '.bindings[].members[]' | grep -E '^deleted:' || echo "NONE at all"

content.text: The `ZSH_VERSION: unbound variable` noise means `set -u` was leaking into gcloud's shell wrapper — and worse, the key counts came back **empty**, which makes those results untrustworthy (a possible false negative on the tombstone check too). I won't trust a check I can't see the raw output of. Let me re-run without the restrictive shell flags and print the actual gcloud output so the results are verifiable, not inferred from a count.

content.text:
I have a complete and honest picture now. Here's where ₢BYAAA (lifecycle-split-establish-rekey) stands after live verification.

## Gate 1 — `canonical-invest`: GREEN, and the fix is provably load-bearing

`5 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped`. The previously-failing `director_divest` step now passes, and the divest transcript shows the Class-C tolerance from `1a66443e` **firing and being needed** this run:

- **repo-scoped** revoke (`roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin`): `getIamPolicy` → 403 at 0s, 3s, 9s, 21s, 41s, 61s (seven "caller-empowerment propagating" 403s) → **200 at 81s** → revoked. Pre-fix, this died on the first 403.
- **project-scope** (3 roles) and **Mason-SA-scope** (1 role) revokes: 200 on first GET — no retry. That confirms the lean call was right: only the AR resource-scope cache lags; the tolerance belongs exactly where you put it.
- Then DELETE 200 → 3×404 "durably gone." H1 end-to-end: revoke live members *first*, then delete.

## Gate 2 — independent gcloud check: a correction, then the real state

**I have to correct myself:** my first tombstone grep reported "NONE" — that was a **false negative**. `set -u` was leaking into gcloud's shell wrapper (`ZSH_VERSION: unbound variable`) and silently killing the call; I trusted a count instead of raw output. Re-run with raw output, the real state is:

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Both canonical SAs present + enabled | ✅ director-canest-dir, retriever-canest-ret |
| Exactly one USER_MANAGED key each | ✅ director 1 (02:21:24Z), retriever 1 (02:20:19Z) |
| `deleted:…?uid=` ghosts naming canonical SAs | ⚠️ **3 director + 3 retriever present** |
| Live SA uids fresh (not ghosted) | ✅ dir `110016831134798782865`, ret `109861987964405347030` — neither in ghost set |

The 6 canonical ghosts are consistent with **pre-fix accumulation** (sweeping existing ghosts is cinched out-of-scope). The transcript proves *this run's* divest revoked every binding before delete, so mechanically it added zero — but **a single post-run snapshot can't separate old ghosts from new ones.** The mechanism is proven; the absolute count is not, on its own, the clean proof the done-criteria wants.

Two more honest notes:

1. **H2's rotation path isn't actually exercised by `canonical-invest`.** Its sequence divests (deletes) each SA *before* investing, so invest always hits the 404→create branch. The "re-invest rotates the key without SA delete/recreate" claim (idempotent 200-preflight → keep uid, rotate key) is a *different* path I haven't directly observed.
2. **Separate finding (likely outside this pace's scope):** there are **7 `governor-*` tombstones**. `governor_mantle` appears to still leak ghosts the way director/retriever divest used to — this pace fixed the keyfile tier (director + retriever), not the governor/mantle tier. Probably an itch, not ₢BYAAA's job, but I didn't want to bury it.

## What would make the proof airtight

I now have a captured baseline (exactly 3+3 distinct ghost uids; live uids `110016…`/`109861…`). The clean confirmations available, both inside the sanctioned standing-depot envelope (no depot levy/unmake):

- **(A) Tombstone delta — proves H1 directly:** re-run `canonical-invest`; its divest will delete today's live instances (`110016…`/`109861…`). If the fix holds, those uids do **not** become ghosts and the count stays 3+3; if broken, they appear. ~3–4 min.
- **(B) H2 rotation — invest-on-standing-SA:** run a director/retriever invest *without* a preceding divest, confirm uniqueId is unchanged and the key id rotated. Proves the no-delete re-key path.

My recommendation: run **(A)** — it turns the strong transcript inference into a direct observation and is the literal "no tombstones" proof. **(B)** if you also want the rotation path nailed.

I have **not** wrapped, and won't without your call. How would you like to close it out — run (A), (A)+(B), or accept the transcript-level mechanism evidence as sufficient? And do you want the governor-tombstone leak captured as an itch?

content.content:
=== the Class-C retry loop as actually committed in rbgi_IAM.sh ===
99:# Three propagation classes are encoded as tolerance pairs:
102:#   (403, "")                 — caller-recently-empowered (resource-scope cache lag)
105:# (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) add the 403 pair. Time-bound on 403 is the
106:# discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial waits
110:zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate() {
120:  while test "$#" -ge 2; do
170:  # Propagation retry — project-scope tolerance: forward/backward
171:  # member-visibility (400 patterns). Class C (403 resource-scope cache lag)
183:  while :; do
196:    # Check for propagation error on GET
197:    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
198:      buc_log_args "${z_label}: getIamPolicy returned ${z_get_code} (propagation delay)"
200:        || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
209:    # Not a propagation error on GET — require success
231:    while :; do
238:      # Check for propagation error on SET — break inner loop to retry outer
239:      if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_set_infix}" "${z_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
240:        buc_log_args "${z_label}: setIamPolicy returned ${z_code} (propagation delay)"
247:        429|500|502|503|504) buc_log_args "Transient ${z_code} at ${z_set_elapsed}s; retry" ;;
256:    # If setIamPolicy succeeded, break outer propagation loop
259:    # setIamPolicy hit propagation error — retry outer loop with fresh getIamPolicy
261:      || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
269:  test "${z_prop_succeeded}" = "1" || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation retry loop exited without success"
278:  # propagates on GCP's clock regardless and cannot be accelerated by polling — so
310:  # Propagation retry — AR repo is resource-scope: member-visibility 400s
311:  # plus caller-recently-empowered 403 from the resource-scope IAM cache.
315:    "403" ""
324:  while :; do
334:    # Check for propagation error on GET
335:    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
336:      buc_log_args "Repo getIamPolicy returned ${z_get_code} (propagation delay)"
338:        || buc_die "Repo IAM: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s"
347:    # Not a propagation error on GET — require success
371:    while :; do
378:      # Check for propagation error on SET — break inner loop to retry outer
379:      if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_set_infix}" "${z_set_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
380:        buc_log_args "Repo setIamPolicy returned ${z_set_code} (propagation delay)"
387:        429|500|502|503|504) buc_log_args "Transient ${z_set_code} at ${z_set_elapsed}s; retry" ;;
396:    # If setIamPolicy succeeded, break outer propagation loop
399:    # setIamPolicy hit propagation error — retry outer loop with fresh getIamPolicy
401:      || buc_die "Repo IAM: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s"
409:  test "${z_prop_succeeded}" = "1" || buc_die "Repo IAM: propagation retry loop exited without success"
445:  # Propagation retry — SA is resource-scope: member-visibility 400s plus
446:  # caller-recently-empowered 403 from the resource-scope IAM cache.
450:    "403" ""
459:  while :; do
470:    # Check for propagation error on GET
471:    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
472:      buc_log_args "SA getIamPolicy returned ${z_code} (propagation delay)"
474:        || buc_die "SA IAM: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s"

=== the Class-C retry loop as actually committed in rbgi_IAM.sh ===
99:# Three propagation classes are encoded as tolerance pairs:
102:#   (403, "")                 — caller-recently-empowered (resource-scope cache lag)
105:# (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) add the 403 pair. Time-bound on 403 is the
106:# discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial waits
110:zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate() {
120:  while test "$#" -ge 2; do
170:  # Propagation retry — project-scope tolerance: forward/backward
171:  # member-visibility (400 patterns). Class C (403 resource-scope cache lag)
183:  while :; do
196:    # Check for propagation error on GET
197:    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
198:      buc_log_args "${z_label}: getIamPolicy returned ${z_get_code} (propagation delay)"
200:        || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
209:    # Not a propagation error on GET — require success
231:    while :; do
238:      # Check for propagation error on SET — break inner loop to retry outer
239:      if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_set_infix}" "${z_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
240:        buc_log_args "${z_label}: setIamPolicy returned ${z_code} (propagation delay)"
247:        429|500|502|503|504) buc_log_args "Transient ${z_code} at ${z_set_elapsed}s; retry" ;;
256:    # If setIamPolicy succeeded, break outer propagation loop
259:    # setIamPolicy hit propagation error — retry outer loop with fresh getIamPolicy
261:      || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
269:  test "${z_prop_succeeded}" = "1" || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation retry loop exited without success"
278:  # propagates on GCP's clock regardless and cannot be accelerated by polling — so
310:  # Propagation retry — AR repo is resource-scope: member-visibility 400s
311:  # plus caller-recently-empowered 403 from the resource-scope IAM cache.
315:    "403" ""
324:  while :; do
334:    # Check for propagation error on GET

409:  test "${z_prop_succeeded}" = "1" || buc_die "Repo IAM: propagation retry loop exited without success"

1	# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)
2	
3	*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
4	crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
5	repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
6	canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*
7	
8	## Headline
9	
10	The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
11	an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
12	lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
13	The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
14	separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
15	hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.
16	
17	## Objects
18	
19	- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
20	  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
21	- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
22	  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
23	- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
24	  a **Resource**.
25	- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
26	  repo**, the **Mason SA**.
27	
28	## Three independent lifecycles
29	
30	1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
31	   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
32	2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
33	   credential; the SA is untouched.*
34	3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
35	   by member = SA.
36	
37	Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
38	kill the old one.
39	
40	## The hole
41	
42	The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
43	**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
44	does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:
45	
46	- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
47	  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
48	  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
49	  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
50	- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
51	  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
52	  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
53	  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.
54	
55	## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)
56	
57	- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
58	  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
59	  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
60	  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
61	  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
62	  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
63	  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
64	- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
65	  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
66	  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
67	  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
68	  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
69	  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
70	  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
71	  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
72	- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
73	  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
74	  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
75	  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
76	  a not-yet-converged view.
77	
78	Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
79	routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
80	crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.
81	
82	## The fix — separate the lifecycles
83	
84	Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:
85	
86	- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
87	  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
88	  already established.
89	- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
90	  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
91	  Bindings.
92	- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
93	  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
94	  rather than every cycle.
95	
96	This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
97	and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:
98	
99	1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
100	   teardown).
101	2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
102	3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
103	4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
104	5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
105	   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
106	   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
107	6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
108	   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
109	   shows the policy is writable).
110	7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
111	   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
112	8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
113	   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.
114	
115	## Footnote: federation
116	
117	The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
118	grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
119	no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
120	**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
121	repaired in place as above.
122	
123	## Evidence appendix
124	
125	- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
126	  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
127	  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
128	- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
129	  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
130	- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
131	  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
132	- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
133	- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
134	  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
135	- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
136	  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
137	  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
138	  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).
139	
140	[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
141	[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies
142

# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)

*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*

## Headline

The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.

## Objects

- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
  a **Resource**.
- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
  repo**, the **Mason SA**.

## Three independent lifecycles

1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
   credential; the SA is untouched.*
3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
   by member = SA.

Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
kill the old one.

## The hole

The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:

- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.

## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)

- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
  a not-yet-converged view.

Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.

## The fix — separate the lifecycles

Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:

- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
  already established.
- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
  Bindings.
- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
  rather than every cycle.

This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:

1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
   teardown).
2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
   shows the policy is writable).
7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.

## Footnote: federation

The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
repaired in place as above.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies

content.text: The grounding memo is thorough — but I notice it documents the propagation races on the **grant/invest** side (DRS-400 create→grant, delete→recreate) and explicitly scopes governor out. It does **not** describe the failure we just hit: a 403 on the **revoke** path during divest because the just-re-mantled governor's `roles/owner` hadn't propagated to the AR repo's resource-scope IAM cache. Let me read the sibling 6/4 memo and the actual tolerance-class machinery to be precise.

content.content:
1	# Dogfight failure — IAM read-after-delete flap defeats `rbuh_poll_until_gone`
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-06-04
4	**Invocation**: `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh`
5	**Suite**: dogfight (`canonical-invest` → `dogfight` fixtures)
6	**Fix commit**: `2202ece57` (this finding's repair; failing run predates it)
7	
8	## Phenomenon (one line)
9	
10	A service account's GET read path flaps **404→200 across replicas after a
11	DELETE**, so a single observed 404 is not durable proof of deletion. This is
12	the inverse of the create-side flap already recorded in
13	`memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` (200→200→404 after create).
14	
15	## Test outcome
16	
17	| Fixture | Case | Result |
18	|---|---|---|
19	| canonical-invest | `rbtdrk_governor_mantle` | passed |
20	| canonical-invest | `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` | **failed** — `invest retriever exit 1` |
21	
22	The suite halted at the second case; `rbtdrk_director_invest` and the dogfight
23	fixture never ran. The canonical-invest fixture is designed to be idempotent:
24	`rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` runs an explicit **divest before invest** (commit
25	`e0d8fb281`), 404-tolerant, so a standing-depot rerun clears the prior SA. That
26	divest **ran and succeeded** — the failure was downstream, in the invest's
27	existence preflight.
28	
29	## Forensic artifacts (theurge trace, reaped-at-risk)
30	
31	Trace dir: `../temp-buk/temp-20260604-112141-44242-735/rbtd/rbtdrk_retriever_invest/`
32	
33	- Depot project: `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`
34	- SA: `retriever-canest-ret@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com`
35	
36	**Divest** (`rbw-arD canest-ret`, 11:21:51) — transcript:
37	
38	```
39	HTTP DELETE .../serviceAccounts/retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200
40	Confirm deletion propagated before any same-name recreate
41	HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # still present, waiting 3s
42	HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # still present, waiting 3s
43	HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 404   # "gone after 6 seconds"  → returns success
44	```
45	
46	**Invest** (`rbw-arI canest-ret`, 11:21:59, ~2s after the divest's lone 404) — transcript:
47	
48	```
49	Preflight: confirm retriever-canest-ret does not already exist
50	HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # flapped back to present
51	buc_die: Service account ... already exists — run the matching GovernorDivests verb first to re-key
52	```
53	
54	## Root cause
55	
56	`rbuh_poll_until_gone` (`rbuh_Http.sh`) declared the SA gone on the **first**
57	404. GCP IAM's SA read path is multi-replica eventually-consistent: a
58	post-DELETE GET flaps 200↔404 for seconds as replicas converge. The poll
59	hit a transient 404 from one replica and returned; the invest's preflight GET
60	~2s later hit a replica still showing the SA (200) and tripped the fail-loud
61	existence preflight (`rbgg_Governor.sh`, `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key`).
62	
63	Note the task's initial hypothesis ("SA left over from a prior run, never
64	divested") was **contradicted by the trace** — the divest ran and confirmed-gone
65	in this very invocation. The defect is the read-flap, not stale state.
66	
67	## Fix
68	
69	Honors `e0d8fb281`'s cinch (idempotency lives in the fixture-level divest;
70	invest stays fail-loud) — the repair is in the divest/poll membrane, preflight
71	untouched:
72	
73	- `rbuh_poll_until_gone` now requires `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` (=3)
74	  **consecutive** 404 observations before declaring durably gone; any
75	  intervening non-404 resets the streak. Total wait still bounded by
76	  `RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC`.
77	- New constant `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` in `rbgc_Constants.sh` +

file.content:
# Dogfight failure — IAM read-after-delete flap defeats `rbuh_poll_until_gone`

**Date**: 2026-06-04
**Invocation**: `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh`
**Suite**: dogfight (`canonical-invest` → `dogfight` fixtures)
**Fix commit**: `2202ece57` (this finding's repair; failing run predates it)

## Phenomenon (one line)

A service account's GET read path flaps **404→200 across replicas after a
DELETE**, so a single observed 404 is not durable proof of deletion. This is
the inverse of the create-side flap already recorded in
`memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` (200→200→404 after create).

## Test outcome

| Fixture | Case | Result |
|---|---|---|
| canonical-invest | `rbtdrk_governor_mantle` | passed |
| canonical-invest | `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` | **failed** — `invest retriever exit 1` |

The suite halted at the second case; `rbtdrk_director_invest` and the dogfight
fixture never ran. The canonical-invest fixture is designed to be idempotent:
`rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` runs an explicit **divest before invest** (commit
`e0d8fb281`), 404-tolerant, so a standing-depot rerun clears the prior SA. That
divest **ran and succeeded** — the failure was downstream, in the invest's
existence preflight.

## Forensic artifacts (theurge trace, reaped-at-risk)

Trace dir: `../temp-buk/temp-20260604-112141-44242-735/rbtd/rbtdrk_retriever_invest/`

- Depot project: `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`
- SA: `retriever-canest-ret@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com`

**Divest** (`rbw-arD canest-ret`, 11:21:51) — transcript:

```
HTTP DELETE .../serviceAccounts/retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200
Confirm deletion propagated before any same-name recreate
HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # still present, waiting 3s
HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # still present, waiting 3s
HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 404   # "gone after 6 seconds"  → returns success
```

**Invest** (`rbw-arI canest-ret`, 11:21:59, ~2s after the divest's lone 404) — transcript:

```
Preflight: confirm retriever-canest-ret does not already exist
HTTP GET .../retriever-canest-ret@... returned code 200   # flapped back to present
buc_die: Service account ... already exists — run the matching GovernorDivests verb first to re-key
```

## Root cause

`rbuh_poll_until_gone` (`rbuh_Http.sh`) declared the SA gone on the **first**
404. GCP IAM's SA read path is multi-replica eventually-consistent: a
post-DELETE GET flaps 200↔404 for seconds as replicas converge. The poll
hit a transient 404 from one replica and returned; the invest's preflight GET
~2s later hit a replica still showing the SA (200) and tripped the fail-loud
existence preflight (`rbgg_Governor.sh`, `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key`).

Note the task's initial hypothesis ("SA left over from a prior run, never
divested") was **contradicted by the trace** — the divest ran and confirmed-gone
in this very invocation. The defect is the read-flap, not stale state.

## Fix

Honors `e0d8fb281`'s cinch (idempotency lives in the fixture-level divest;
invest stays fail-loud) — the repair is in the divest/poll membrane, preflight
untouched:

- `rbuh_poll_until_gone` now requires `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` (=3)
  **consecutive** 404 observations before declaring durably gone; any
  intervening non-404 resets the streak. Total wait still bounded by
  `RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC`.
- New constant `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` in `rbgc_Constants.sh` +
  `rbuh` kindle-sentinel validation.
- Spec drift repaired: RBSRD / RBSDD "Confirm Deletion Propagated" step now
  states the consecutive-404 requirement; RBSCIP gains the delete-side flap as
  a documented propagation behavior (this memo is its evidence row).

## Verification

`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` green twice consecutively (proves both the
immediate resolution and idempotency from a dirty post-success state).

file.content:
zrbgi_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBGI_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbgi not kindled - call zrbgi_kindle first"
}

# Classify an HTTP response against a caller-supplied tolerance list.
# Each tolerance is a (code, body-glob) pair passed as two positional args
# after the infix and code. An empty body-glob matches any response body
# for that code — short-circuits without loading the error message.
#
# Three propagation classes are encoded as tolerance pairs:
#   (400, "*does not exist*") — forward member-visibility lag
#   (400, "*is not deleted*") — backward member-visibility lag
#   (403, "")                 — caller-recently-empowered (resource-scope cache lag)
#
# Project-scope sites declare only the two 400 patterns; resource-scope sites
# (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) add the 403 pair. Time-bound on 403 is the
# discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial waits
# the budget and fails cleanly.
#
# Returns 0 (true) if response matches any tolerance pair, 1 otherwise.
zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate() {
  local -r z_infix="${1}"
  local -r z_code="${2}"
  shift 2

  local z_err_msg=""
  local z_err_loaded=0
  local z_tol_code=""
  local z_tol_glob=""

  while test "$#" -ge 2; do
    z_tol_code="${1}"
    z_tol_glob="${2}"
    shift 2

    test "${z_code}" = "${z_tol_code}" || continue

    test -n "${z_tol_glob}" || return 0

    if test "${z_err_loaded}" = "0"; then
      z_err_msg=$(rbge_error_message_capture "${z_infix}") || z_err_msg=""
      z_err_loaded=1
    fi

    case "${z_err_msg}" in
      ${z_tol_glob}) return 0 ;;
    esac
  done

  return 1
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbgi_*)

# Add a project-scoped IAM role binding with optimistic concurrency and strong read-back.
rbgi_add_project_iam_role() {
  zrbgi_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  local -r z_label="${2:-}"
  local -r z_resource="${3:-}"  # resource_base: Base resource URL
  local -r z_role="${4:-}"
  local -r z_member="${5:-}"
  local -r z_parent_infix="${6:-}"

  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Token required"
  buc_log_args "Using admin token (value not logged)"

  local -r z_resource_path="${z_resource#/}"  # strip leading slash if present
  local -r z_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V1}/${z_resource_path}"
  local -r z_get_url="${z_base}${RBGC_CRM_GET_IAM_POLICY_SUFFIX}"
  local -r z_set_url="${z_base}${RBGC_CRM_SET_IAM_POLICY_SUFFIX}"

  test -n "${z_resource}" || buc_die "resource required"
  test -n "${z_role}"     || buc_die "role required"
  test -n "${z_member}"   || buc_die "member required"

  buc_log_args "${z_label}: add ${z_member} to ${z_role}"

  # Propagation retry — project-scope tolerance: forward/backward
  # member-visibility (400 patterns). Class C (403 resource-scope cache lag)
  # does not apply at project scope.
  local -ra z_tolerance=(
    "400" "*does not exist*"
    "400" "*is not deleted*"
  )
  local z_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC}
  local z_prop_elapsed=0
  local -r z_prop_deadline=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC}
  local z_prop_attempt=0
  local z_prop_succeeded=0

  while :; do
    z_prop_attempt=$((z_prop_attempt + 1))

    buc_log_args "1) GET policy (v3) [attempt ${z_prop_attempt}]"
    buc_log_args "GET_POLICY_URL_DEBUG z_resource:${z_resource} z_get_url:${z_get_url}"
    local z_get_body="${ZRBGI_PREFIX}${z_parent_infix}_get_body.json"
    local z_get_infix="${z_parent_infix}-get-${z_prop_elapsed}s"
    printf '%s\n' '{"options":{"requestedPolicyVersion":3}}' > "${z_get_body}"
    rbuh_json "POST" "${z_get_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_body}"

    local z_get_code=""
    z_get_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${z_get_infix}") || buc_die "No HTTP code from getIamPolicy"

    # Check for propagation error on GET
    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
      buc_log_args "${z_label}: getIamPolicy returned ${z_get_code} (propagation delay)"
      test "${z_prop_elapsed}" -lt "${z_prop_deadline}" \
        || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
      buc_log_args "Retry ${z_prop_attempt} at ${z_prop_elapsed}s (next delay ${z_prop_delay}s)"
      sleep "${z_prop_delay}"
      z_prop_elapsed=$((z_prop_elapsed + z_prop_delay))
      z_prop_delay=$((z_prop_delay * 2))
      test "${z_prop_delay}" -le "${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}" || z_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}
      continue
    fi

    # Not a propagation error on GET — require success
    rbuh_require_ok "${z_label} (get policy)" "${z_get_infix}"

    buc_log_args 'Extract etag; require non-empty'
    local z_etag=""
    z_etag=$(rbuh_json_field_capture "${z_get_infix}" ".etag") || buc_die "Missing etag"
    test -n "${z_etag}" || buc_die "Empty etag"

    buc_log_args "Using etag ${z_etag}"

    buc_log_args '2) Build new policy JSON in temp (bindings unique; version=3; keep etag)'

1	==== Decision
2	
3	IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
4	connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
5	readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.
6	
7	Three propagation classes are recognized by the IAM grant harness in
8	`zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`, all sharing the same exponential-backoff
9	budget defined by the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` constants:
10	
11	* **Class A — forward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
12	  calls fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist" when a newly-created service
13	  account is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.
14	* **Class B — backward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
15	  calls fail with HTTP 400 "is not deleted" when a recently-deleted service
16	  account is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
17	* **Class C — caller-recently-empowered**: `getIamPolicy` / `setIamPolicy`
18	  on a resource-scope target (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) returns HTTP 403
19	  because the caller's recently-granted role (e.g. fresh `roles/owner` on a
20	  newly-levied depot) has not yet reached the resource-scope IAM cache,
21	  even though it has reached project- and SA-scope caches.
22	
23	Class C is time-bounded only — no body fingerprinting. The bound itself is
24	the discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial
25	waits the budget and fails cleanly. The same model is already proven in
26	{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}. Resource-scope grant sites declare 403 tolerance
27	with an empty body-glob; project-scope sites do not.
28	
29	* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
30	  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
31	  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
32	* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
33	  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
34	  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
35	  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
36	  `rbuh_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
37	  grant IAM bindings.
38	
39	* **SA keys subresource propagation**: After
40	  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[serviceAccounts.create]
41	  returns 200, the keys subresource read path
42	  (link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list[serviceAccounts.keys.list])
43	  can lag the SA email path independently — empirically observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap where
44	  both the SA GET probe and a keys subresource `rbuh_poll_until_ok` probe return 200 while the real
45	  `keys.list` call immediately returns 404 on the same URL.  Mitigation: retry the actual `keys.list`
46	  call on HTTP 404 using the same backoff budget as step 7 keys.create (`RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_*`) —
47	  same underlying physics (subresource visibility after parent creation).  A separate
48	  `rbuh_poll_until_ok` proxy probe before `keys.list` was found insufficient: it returned 200 in the
49	  same flap window that the real call observed 404, exemplifying why proxy probes are not retained as
50	  correctness gates (see "What Dies" below).  See evidence row below.
51	
52	* **SA delete read-path flap**: The inverse of the create-side flap above.  After
53	  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/delete[serviceAccounts.delete]
54	  returns 200, the SA email GET read path flaps non-monotonically 404→200 across replicas while the
55	  deletion propagates — so a single 404 is not durable proof the account is gone.  A same-name recreate
56	  ({rbtgo_retriever_invest} / {rbtgo_director_invest}) racing a still-lagging replica then trips the
57	  invest existence preflight (200 → fail-loud "already exists").  Empirically observed where
58	  `rbuh_poll_until_gone` saw GET 200,200,404 and declared gone at 6s, then the recreate's preflight GET
59	  returned 200 two seconds later.  Mitigation: `rbuh_poll_until_gone` requires `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK`
60	  consecutive 404 observations before declaring the resource durably gone; any intervening 200 resets the
61	  streak, and the total wait stays bounded by `RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC`.  Same underlying physics as the
62	  create-side flap (multi-replica read convergence after a parent mutation).  See evidence row below.
63	
64	* **Lifecycle split relocates both SA-churn races (keyfile tier)**: the
65	  idempotent establish / re-key split (see {rbtgo_retriever_invest} and
66	  {rbtgo_director_invest}) changes *when* the two races can fire.  The
67	  **create→grant race** (Class A forward member-visibility, plus the org's
68	  Domain-Restricted-Sharing lag on a brand-new SA member) now fires only at
69	  *first establish* — once per identity, off the re-key hot path — because a
70	  standing-depot re-invest rotates the key without minting a new SA.  The
71	  **delete→recreate race** (the SA delete read-path flap above) is
72	  *structurally eliminated* for re-key: the SA is no longer deleted and
73	  recreated, so there is no same-name recreate to race a lagging replica.  The
74	  `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` mitigation survives only for the path that still
75	  deletes-then-reinvests — a genuine teardown ({rbtgo_retriever_divest} /
76	  {rbtgo_director_divest}) followed later by a fresh invest — now rare rather
77	  than per-cycle.  See
78	  `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`.
79	
80	==== Rationale
81	
82	The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
83	When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
84	a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
85	the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.
86	
87	Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:
88	
89	* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
90	  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.

file.content:
==== Decision

IAM propagation delays for Cloud Build v2 connection creation are handled by
connection-creation retry with exponential backoff, not by an impersonation
readiness gate or fixed sleep delays.

Three propagation classes are recognized by the IAM grant harness in
`zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate`, all sharing the same exponential-backoff
budget defined by the `RBGC_PROPAGATION_*` constants:

* **Class A — forward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "does not exist" when a newly-created service
  account is not yet visible to the IAM policy service as a valid member.
* **Class B — backward member-visibility**: `setIamPolicy` and `getIamPolicy`
  calls fail with HTTP 400 "is not deleted" when a recently-deleted service
  account is still referenced in existing policy bindings.
* **Class C — caller-recently-empowered**: `getIamPolicy` / `setIamPolicy`
  on a resource-scope target (AR repo, SA, bucket, secret) returns HTTP 403
  because the caller's recently-granted role (e.g. fresh `roles/owner` on a
  newly-levied depot) has not yet reached the resource-scope IAM cache,
  even though it has reached project- and SA-scope caches.

Class C is time-bounded only — no body fingerprinting. The bound itself is
the discriminator: real propagation succeeds within budget, real denial
waits the budget and fails cleanly. The same model is already proven in
{rbtgo_access_jwt_probe}. Resource-scope grant sites declare 403 tolerance
with an empty body-glob; project-scope sites do not.

* **Secret access readiness**: The CB v2 connection creation LRO itself serves as the propagation test.
  If the Cloud Build Service Agent cannot yet access the secrets (IAM grant not propagated), the LRO fails.
  The caller retries with exponential backoff, deleting the zombie connection resource between attempts.
* **Secret resource propagation**: `getIamPolicy` on a newly created secret may return HTTP 404 while
  the Secret Manager resource propagates across Google's control plane.  The API documents that
  `getIamPolicy` returns an empty policy (HTTP 200) when no policy has been set — so 404 indicates
  the resource itself is not yet visible, not that the policy is absent.  After creating secrets,
  `rbuh_poll_until_ok` polls each secret's `getIamPolicy` endpoint until 200 before proceeding to
  grant IAM bindings.

* **SA keys subresource propagation**: After
  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[serviceAccounts.create]
  returns 200, the keys subresource read path
  (link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list[serviceAccounts.keys.list])
  can lag the SA email path independently — empirically observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap where
  both the SA GET probe and a keys subresource `rbuh_poll_until_ok` probe return 200 while the real
  `keys.list` call immediately returns 404 on the same URL.  Mitigation: retry the actual `keys.list`
  call on HTTP 404 using the same backoff budget as step 7 keys.create (`RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_*`) —
  same underlying physics (subresource visibility after parent creation).  A separate
  `rbuh_poll_until_ok` proxy probe before `keys.list` was found insufficient: it returned 200 in the
  same flap window that the real call observed 404, exemplifying why proxy probes are not retained as
  correctness gates (see "What Dies" below).  See evidence row below.

* **SA delete read-path flap**: The inverse of the create-side flap above.  After
  link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/delete[serviceAccounts.delete]
  returns 200, the SA email GET read path flaps non-monotonically 404→200 across replicas while the
  deletion propagates — so a single 404 is not durable proof the account is gone.  A same-name recreate
  ({rbtgo_retriever_invest} / {rbtgo_director_invest}) racing a still-lagging replica then trips the
  invest existence preflight (200 → fail-loud "already exists").  Empirically observed where
  `rbuh_poll_until_gone` saw GET 200,200,404 and declared gone at 6s, then the recreate's preflight GET
  returned 200 two seconds later.  Mitigation: `rbuh_poll_until_gone` requires `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK`
  consecutive 404 observations before declaring the resource durably gone; any intervening 200 resets the
  streak, and the total wait stays bounded by `RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC`.  Same underlying physics as the
  create-side flap (multi-replica read convergence after a parent mutation).  See evidence row below.

* **Lifecycle split relocates both SA-churn races (keyfile tier)**: the
  idempotent establish / re-key split (see {rbtgo_retriever_invest} and
  {rbtgo_director_invest}) changes *when* the two races can fire.  The
  **create→grant race** (Class A forward member-visibility, plus the org's
  Domain-Restricted-Sharing lag on a brand-new SA member) now fires only at
  *first establish* — once per identity, off the re-key hot path — because a
  standing-depot re-invest rotates the key without minting a new SA.  The
  **delete→recreate race** (the SA delete read-path flap above) is
  *structurally eliminated* for re-key: the SA is no longer deleted and
  recreated, so there is no same-name recreate to race a lagging replica.  The
  `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` mitigation survives only for the path that still
  deletes-then-reinvests — a genuine teardown ({rbtgo_retriever_divest} /
  {rbtgo_director_divest}) followed later by a fresh invest — now rare rather
  than per-cycle.  See
  `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`.

==== Rationale

The connection creation LRO tests the actual operation, not a proxy.
When the Cloud Build Service Agent accesses resources requiring recently-granted permissions,
a successful LRO proves end-to-end readiness: the IAM grant has propagated,
the service agent identity is active, and the resource content is valid.

Fixed `sleep` delays ("sleep and pray") are unreliable:

* Google's IAM propagation documentation states changes "typically take less than 2 minutes"
  but provides no guaranteed upper bound.
